title_en: Ash-Shafi'i: Founder of the School and the Science of Legal Theory
title_ar: الشافعي: مؤسس المذهب وعلم أصول الفقه
content_en:
The biography of ash-Shafi'i that opens Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra is one of the work's most important sections and one of the richest biographical accounts of any Islamic legal scholar. Ash-Shafi'i was not merely the founder of a legal school but one of the most original thinkers in the history of Islamic scholarship, and as-Subki's biography attempts to convey both his intellectual achievements and his personal qualities.

Muhammad ibn Idris ash-Shafi'i was born in Gaza (or possibly Mecca, according to different accounts) in 150 AH, the year of Abu Hanifa's death. He was raised in Mecca and memorized the Quran as a child. He studied with Malik ibn Anas in Medina, learning the Madinese legal tradition and the Muwatta. He traveled to Iraq, where he encountered the Hanafi tradition and the rationalist legal methodology associated with Abu Hanifa's disciples. He subsequently lived in Yemen, returned to Baghdad, and eventually settled in Egypt, where he died in 204 AH.

This intellectual biography — training under both the great traditions of his time and then synthesizing them in a new framework — is directly relevant to ash-Shafi'i's most significant intellectual contribution: the founding of the science of usul al-fiqh (legal theory or the principles of jurisprudence). His Risalah fi Usul al-Fiqh was the first systematic treatise on the sources of Islamic law and the rules for deriving legal rulings from them. It established the framework — Quran, Sunnah, consensus (ijma'), and analogical reasoning (qiyas) — within which all subsequent Islamic legal theory has operated.

As-Subki's biography discusses ash-Shafi'i's Risalah and its intellectual significance, presenting it as an achievement that transcended any single legal school. Ash-Shafi'i's articulation of usul al-fiqh provided a meta-framework for understanding the diversity of legal opinion within the Sunni tradition — showing how different schools could reach different conclusions from the same sources while all operating within a legitimate methodological framework.

The biography also preserves ash-Shafi'i's famous statements about knowledge, piety, and the proper conduct of scholars — statements that became part of the Shafi'i school's identity and that are cited across the tradition as guides to scholarly virtue.
